chapter.title: Thermodynamics
chapter.summary:
This chapter introduces thermodynamics, the study of energy transformations in chemical and physical processes. It begins by defining thermodynamic terms like system, surroundings, state functions (internal energy, enthalpy), and types of systems (open, closed, isolated). The First Law of Thermodynamics ($\Delta U = q + w$) is introduced, linking internal energy change to heat and work.

The chapter then explores pressure-volume work, enthalpy ($H = U + pV$), heat capacity, and calorimetry for measuring $\Delta U$ and $\Delta H$. Hess’s Law is presented as a tool for calculating reaction enthalpies using different reaction pathways. Concepts of bond enthalpies, lattice enthalpy, and enthalpy of solution are discussed for various reaction types.

The concept of spontaneity is introduced, leading to a discussion of entropy ($S$) as a measure of disorder and the Second Law of Thermodynamics ($\Delta S_{total} > 0$ for spontaneous processes in isolated systems). Finally, Gibbs Free Energy ($G = H - TS$) is defined as a criterion for spontaneity at constant temperature and pressure ($\Delta G < 0$), and its relationship to the equilibrium constant ($K$) is explored ($\Delta G^\ominus = -RT \ln K$). The chapter concludes by examining the effect of temperature on spontaneity and the applications of Gibbs energy in predicting equilibrium conditions. The chapter progresses from defining fundamental concepts and laws to applying them for quantitative calculations of thermodynamic properties and predictions of reaction spontaneity.

subsections.title: Introduction to Thermodynamics
subsections.content_text: Thermodynamics is the study of energy transformations. Chemical energy stored in molecules (like methane or coal) can be released as heat during reactions or used to do mechanical or electrical work. The laws of thermodynamics deal with energy changes of macroscopic systems involving a large number of molecules rather than microscopic systems. Thermodynamics is not concerned with the rate or mechanism of energy transformations, but focuses on the initial and final states of a system undergoing change. These laws apply only when a system is in equilibrium or moves from one equilibrium state to another. Macroscopic properties like pressure and temperature do not change with time for a system in equilibrium.

subsections.title: The System and the Surroundings
subsections.content_text: A system in thermodynamics refers to that part of the universe in which observations are made, and the remaining universe constitutes the surroundings. The surroundings include everything other than the system that can interact with it. Usually, the region of space in the neighborhood of the system constitutes its surroundings. The relationship is expressed as: Universe = System + Surroundings. The system is separated from the surroundings by a boundary, which can be real or imaginary, designed to control and track the movement of matter and energy.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.1
diagrams.description: A diagram showing a beaker containing a reaction mixture as the 'System', with the surrounding space labeled as 'Surroundings', separated by the beaker's wall.

subsections.title: Types of Systems: Open and Closed
subsections.content_text:
Systems are classified according to the movement of matter and energy: 
1. Open System: There is an exchange of both energy and matter between the system and surroundings. An example is reactants in an open beaker where the boundary is an imaginary surface enclosing the beaker.
2. Closed System: There is no exchange of matter, but exchange of energy is possible between the system and surroundings. An example is a closed vessel made of conducting material like copper or steel.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.2 (a) and (b)
diagrams.description: Diagram (a) shows an open system with arrows indicating both matter and energy (heat/work) crossing the boundary. Diagram (b) shows a closed system where only energy crosses the boundary, while matter remains inside.

subsections.title: Isolated System
subsections.content_text: In an isolated system, there is no exchange of energy or matter between the system and the surroundings. The presence of reactants in a thermos flask or any other closed insulated vessel is an example of an isolated system.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.2 (c)
diagrams.description: Diagram (c) shows an isolated system where neither matter nor energy can cross the boundary into the surroundings.

subsections.title: The State of the System
subsections.content_text: The state of a thermodynamic system is described by its measurable macroscopic (bulk) properties. These include pressure ($p$), volume ($V$), temperature ($T$), and amount ($n$) of substance. These variables are called state variables or state functions because their values depend only on the current state of the system and not on how that state was reached. To define the state of a system completely, it is not necessary to define all properties, as a certain minimum number of independent properties can fix the values of others.

subsections.title: Internal Energy as a State Function
subsections.content_text:
Internal energy ($U$) represents the total energy of a system, which may be chemical, electrical, mechanical, or any other type. It is a state function. The internal energy of a system changes when: 
1. Heat passes into or out of the system.
2. Work is done on or by the system.
3. Matter enters or leaves the system.

subsections.title: Work and Adiabatic Processes
subsections.content_text: Consider a change in internal energy by doing work. If a system is contained in an insulated vessel (like a thermos flask) that does not allow exchange of heat between the system and surroundings, the boundary is called an adiabatic wall and the process is called an adiabatic process. In an adiabatic process, the heat exchange $q = 0$. Let the initial state of the system be state A with temperature $T_A$ and internal energy $U_A$. We can change the state of the system in two different ways One way: We do some mechanical work, say $1\text{ kJ}$, by rotating a set of small paddles and thereby churning water. Let the new state be called B state and its temperature, as $T_B$. It is found that $T_B > T_A$ and the change in temperature, $\Delta T = T_B - T_A$. Let the internal energy of the system in state B be $U_B$ and the change in internal energy, $\Delta U = U_B - U_A$. Second way: We now do an equal amount (i.e., $1\text{ kJ}$) electrical work with the help of an immersion rod and note down the temperature change. We find that the change in temperature is same as in the earlier case, say, $T_B - T_A$. In fact, the experiments in the above manner were done by J. P. Joule between 1840-50 and he was able to show that a given amount of work done on the system, no matter how it was done (irrespective of path) produced the same change of state, as measured by the change in the temperature of the system.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.3
diagrams.description: An adiabatic system depicted as a cylinder with insulated walls, showing matter and energy inside with an arrow indicating $q=0$, meaning no heat transfer through the boundary.

subsections.title: Internal Energy as a State Function
subsections.content_text: Based on Joule's experiments, it is appropriate to define a quantity, the internal energy $U$, whose value is characteristic of the state of a system. The adiabatic work, $w_{ad}$, required to bring about a change of state is equal to the difference between the value of $U$ in one state and that in another state, $\Delta U$ i.e., $\Delta U = U_2 - U_1 = w_{ad}$. Therefore, internal energy, $U$, of the system is a state function.

subsections.title: IUPAC Sign Convention for Work
subsections.content_text: By conventions of IUPAC in chemical thermodynamics, the positive sign expresses that $w_{ad}$ is positive when work is done on the system and the internal energy of system increases. Similarly, if the work is done by the system, $w_{ad}$ will be negative because internal energy of the system decreases. Note: Earlier, a negative sign was assigned when the work is done on the system and a positive sign when the work is done by the system. This is still followed in physics books, although IUPAC has recommended the use of the new sign convention.

subsections.title: Temperature and Volume as State Functions
subsections.content_text: Other familiar state functions include $V, p$, and $T$. For example, if we bring a change in temperature of the system from $25^\circ\text{C}$ to $35^\circ\text{C}$, the change in temperature is $35^\circ\text{C} - 25^\circ\text{C} = +10^\circ\text{C}$, whether we go straight up to $35^\circ\text{C}$ or we cool the system for a few degrees, then take the system to the final temperature. Thus, $T$ is a state function and the change in temperature is independent of the route taken. Similarly, the volume of water in a pond is a state function, because change in volume of its water is independent of the route by which water is filled in the pond, either by rain or by tubewell or by both.

subsections.title: Heat as a Mode of Energy Transfer
subsections.content_text: We can also change the internal energy of a system by transfer of heat from the surroundings to the system or vice-versa without expenditure of work. This exchange of energy, which is a result of temperature difference, is called heat, $q$. Consider bringing about the same change in temperature by transfer of heat through thermally conducting walls instead of adiabatic walls. For example, water at temperature $T_A$ in a container made of copper is enclosed in a huge heat reservoir at temperature $T_B$. The heat absorbed by the system (water), $q$, can be measured in terms of temperature difference, $T_B - T_A$. In this case change in internal energy, $\Delta U = q$, when no work is done at constant volume.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.4
diagrams.description: A schematic diagram of a thermodynamic system containing matter, enclosed by a boundary that allows energy transfer as heat from the surroundings. An arrow labeled 'energy as heat' points from the surroundings into the system.

subsections.title: IUPAC Sign Convention for Heat
subsections.content_text: By conventions of IUPAC in chemical thermodynamics, $q$ is positive when heat is transferred from the surroundings to the system and the internal energy of the system increases. Conversely, $q$ is negative when heat is transferred from the system to the surroundings, resulting in a decrease of the internal energy of the system.

subsections.title: The First Law of Thermodynamics
subsections.content_text: In the general case where a change of state is brought about both by doing work and by transfer of heat, the change in internal energy is: $\Delta U = q + w$ (Eq. 5.1). For a given change in state, $q$ and $w$ can vary depending on the path, but their sum $\Delta U$ depends only on the initial and final states. If there is no transfer of energy as heat or work (isolated system), then $w = 0, q = 0$, and $\Delta U = 0$. Equation 5.1 is the mathematical statement of the first law of thermodynamics, which states: 'The energy of an isolated system is constant.' It is also known as the law of conservation of energy. Note: Unlike volume, which has an absolute value, we can only measure changes in internal energy, $\Delta U$.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Internal Energy Changes
subsections.content_text: This subsection applies the first law of thermodynamics to determine internal energy changes and system types based on heat and work interactions.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.1
worked_examples.problem: Express the change in internal energy of a system when (i) No heat is absorbed by the system from the surroundings, but work (w) is done on the system. What type of wall does the system have? (ii) No work is done on the system, but q amount of heat is taken out from the system and given to the surroundings. What type of wall does the system have? (iii) w amount of work is done by the system and q amount of heat is supplied to the system. What type of system would it be?
worked_examples.solution: (i) $\Delta U = w_{ad}$, wall is adiabatic. (ii) $\Delta U = -q$, thermally conducting walls. (iii) $\Delta U = q - w$, closed system.

subsections.title: Pressure-Volume Work
subsections.content_text: Many chemical reactions involve mechanical work, specifically pressure-volume ($PV$) work. Consider a cylinder containing one mole of an ideal gas fitted with a frictionless piston. The initial volume is $V_i$ and internal pressure is $p$. If external pressure $p_{ex}$ is greater than $p$, the piston moves inward until the internal pressure equals $p_{ex}$.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.5 (a)
diagrams.description: A pV-plot and a cylinder diagram illustrating work done during compression at constant external pressure $p_{ex}$. The graph shows a shaded rectangular area representing work $w = p_{ex} \Delta V$.

subsections.title: Derivation of Work in Compression
subsections.content_text: If the piston moves a distance $l$ with cross-sectional area $A$, the volume change is $\Delta V = (V_f - V_i)$. Force on the piston is $F = p_{ex} \cdot A$. Work done $w = \text{force} \times \text{distance} = p_{ex} \cdot A \cdot l$. Since $A \cdot l = -\Delta V$, we get $w = -p_{ex} \Delta V = -p_{ex}(V_f - V_i)$ (Eq. 5.2). The negative sign ensures $w$ is positive for compression (where $\Delta V$ is negative), consistent with work being done on the system.

subsections.title: Work in Non-constant Pressure (Finite Steps)
subsections.content_text: If the external pressure is not constant but changes in finite steps, the total work done on the gas is the sum of work in each step: $w = -\sum p \Delta V$. This is represented by the shaded area in a $pV$-plot showing multiple rectangular steps.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.5 (b)
diagrams.description: A pV-plot showing work done when pressure is not constant but changes in finite steps during compression. The work is represented by the sum of the areas of several shaded rectangles.

subsections.title: Reversible Processes and Work Integration
subsections.content_text: A process is reversible if it can be reversed at any moment by an infinitesimal change, proceeding through a series of equilibrium states. For such a process, $p_{ex} = p_{in} \pm dp$. The work done is calculated by integration: $w = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} p_{ex} dV$ (Eq. 5.3). A reversible process proceeds infinitely slowly such that the system and surroundings are always in near equilibrium.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.5 (c)
diagrams.description: A pV-plot showing work done under reversible conditions where pressure changes in infinite steps. The work is represented by the shaded area under a smooth curve.

subsections.title: Irreversible Processes and Work Relation
subsections.content_text: In chemistry, many problems are solved by relating the work term to the internal pressure of the system. Under reversible conditions, the work done can be related to the internal pressure ($p_{in}$) by expressing the external pressure as $p_{ex} = p_{in} \pm dp$. The work is then given by: $$w_{rev} = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} p_{ex} dV = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} (p_{in} \pm dp) dV$$ Since the product of two infinitesimal quantities ($dp \times dV$) is extremely small, it can be neglected, simplifying the expression to: $$w_{rev} = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} p_{in} dV$$

subsections.title: Reversible Work Derivation for Ideal Gas
subsections.content_text: For $n$ moles of an ideal gas, the internal pressure $p_{in}$ (simply written as $p$) can be expressed using the ideal gas equation $pV = nRT$, which gives $p = \frac{nRT}{V}$. At a constant temperature (isothermal process), the work done during a reversible expansion or compression is: $$w_{rev} = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} nRT \frac{dV}{V} = -nRT \ln \frac{V_f}{V_i}$$ Converting the natural logarithm to base 10, the formula becomes: $$w_{rev} = -2.303 nRT \log \frac{V_f}{V_i}$$

subsections.title: Free Expansion
subsections.content_text:
Free expansion is defined as the expansion of a gas into a vacuum, where the external pressure ($p_{ex}$) is zero. Since $w = -p_{ex} \Delta V$, no work is done during free expansion ($w = 0$), regardless of whether the process is reversible or irreversible.
For the isothermal expansion ($T = \text{constant}$) of an ideal gas into a vacuum, $w = 0$ because $p_{ex} = 0$. Joule determined experimentally that $q = 0$ for such a process; therefore, according to the first law ($\Delta U = q + w$), $\Delta U = 0$. The first law equation $\Delta U = q + w$ can be expressed for different isothermal conditions as follows: 1. For isothermal irreversible change: $q = -w = p_{ex}(V_f - V_i)$. 2. For isothermal reversible change: $q = -w = nRT \ln \frac{V_f}{V_i} = 2.303 nRT \log \frac{V_f}{V_i}$. For an adiabatic change, $q = 0$, and thus $\Delta U = w_{ad}$.

subsections.title: Worked Examples: Isothermal Expansion
subsections.content_text: The following examples demonstrate the calculation of heat and work for an ideal gas undergoing expansion under different conditions: into a vacuum, against a constant external pressure, and reversibly.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.2
worked_examples.problem: Two litres of an ideal gas at a pressure of 10 atm expands isothermally at $25 ^\circ\text{C}$ into a vacuum until its total volume is 10 litres. How much heat is absorbed and how much work is done in the expansion?
worked_examples.solution: We have $q = -w = p_{ex}(10 - 2) = 0(8) = 0$. No work is done; no heat is absorbed.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.3
worked_examples.problem: Consider the same expansion, but this time against a constant external pressure of 1 atm.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.4
worked_examples.problem: Consider the expansion given in problem 5.2, for 1 mol of an ideal gas conducted reversibly.

subsections.title: Enthalpy as a State Function
subsections.content_text: Most chemical reactions are performed in open vessels at constant atmospheric pressure. Under these conditions, the heat absorbed ($q_p$) is equal to the change in a new state function called enthalpy ($H$). The term enthalpy comes from the Greek word 'enthalpien', meaning to warm or heat content. It is defined as: $$H = U + pV$$ Since internal energy ($U$), pressure ($p$), and volume ($V$) are all state functions, enthalpy ($H$) is also a state function. Consequently, the change in enthalpy ($\Delta H$) is independent of the path taken between states.

subsections.title: Derivation of Enthalpy Change at Constant Pressure
subsections.content_text: At constant pressure, the first law of thermodynamics can be written as $\Delta U = q_p - p\Delta V$. For a change from state 1 to state 2: $$U_2 - U_1 = q_p - p(V_2 - V_1)$$ Rearranging the terms gives: $$q_p = (U_2 + pV_2) - (U_1 + pV_1)$$ Substituting $H = U + pV$, we get: $$q_p = H_2 - H_1 = \Delta H$$ Thus, the heat absorbed by the system at constant pressure is equal to the enthalpy change. For finite changes at constant pressure, the relation is: $$\Delta H = \Delta U + p\Delta V$$

subsections.title: Exothermic and Endothermic Reactions
subsections.content_text: The enthalpy change $\Delta H$ characterizes the heat flow in chemical reactions. $\Delta H$ is negative for exothermic reactions, which release heat to the surroundings. Conversely, $\Delta H$ is positive for endothermic reactions, which absorb heat from the surroundings. It is important to note that at constant volume ($\Delta V = 0$), $\Delta U = q_v$, and the enthalpy change becomes $\Delta H = \Delta U = q_v$. The subscript $v$ in $q_v$ denotes that heat is supplied at constant volume.

subsections.title: Enthalpy Change for Gaseous Reactions
subsections.content_text: For reactions involving gases, volume changes can be significant. If $V_A$ is the total volume of gaseous reactants and $V_B$ is the total volume of gaseous products, with $n_A$ and $n_B$ being their respective mole counts, the ideal gas law at constant temperature and pressure gives: $$pV_A = n_A RT \text{ and } pV_B = n_B RT$$ Subtracting these gives: $$p(V_B - V_A) = (n_B - n_A)RT$$ $$p\Delta V = \Delta n_g RT$$ where $\Delta n_g$ is the number of moles of gaseous products minus the number of moles of gaseous reactants. Substituting this into the enthalpy equation: $$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$$ This equation is used to calculate $\Delta H$ from $\Delta U$ and vice versa.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Enthalpy of Vaporization
subsections.content_text: This example illustrates how to calculate the internal energy change from the molar enthalpy change for a phase transition involving a gas.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.5
worked_examples.problem: If water vapour is assumed to be a perfect gas, molar enthalpy change for vapourisation of 1 mol of water at 1 bar and $100 ^\circ\text{C}$ is $41 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Calculate the internal energy change, when 1 mol of water is vapourised at 1 bar pressure and $100 ^\circ\text{C}$.
worked_examples.solution: The change is $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \to \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$. $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ or $\Delta U = \Delta H - \Delta n_g RT$. Substituting the values: $\Delta n_g = 1 - 0 = 1$. $\Delta U = 41.00 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} - 1 \times 8.3 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1} \times 373 \text{ K} = 41.00 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} - 3.096 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} = 37.904 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Definitions and Examples
subsections.content_text: Thermodynamic properties are divided into two categories: extensive and intensive. An extensive property is one whose value depends on the quantity or size of matter present in the system. Examples include mass, volume, internal energy, enthalpy, and heat capacity. An intensive property is independent of the amount of matter present. Examples include temperature, density, and pressure.

subsections.title: Molar Properties and System Partitioning
subsections.content_text: A molar property, $\chi_m = \frac{\chi}{n}$, is the value of an extensive property $\chi$ for 1 mole of the substance and is an intensive property. For example, molar volume ($V_m$) and molar heat capacity ($C_m$) are intensive. The distinction is clear when considering a gas at volume $V$ and temperature $T$. If the system is partitioned into two equal parts, the volume of each part is $V/2$ (extensive), but the temperature remains $T$ (intensive).

diagrams.label: Fig 5.6(a)
diagrams.description: A rectangular container representing a gas system with volume V and temperature T.

diagrams.label: Fig 5.6(b)
diagrams.description: The same container divided by a central partition into two equal halves, each with volume V/2 and temperature T, illustrating that volume is extensive and temperature is intensive.

subsections.title: Definition and Heat Transfer Relation
subsections.content_text: Heat capacity ($C$) is a measure of the heat required to raise the temperature of a system. The heat transferred ($q$) is proportional to the temperature change ($\Delta T$), expressed as: $$q = \text{coeff} \times \Delta T$$ The coefficient $C$ is called the heat capacity. Its magnitude depends on the size, composition, and nature of the system. We can write: $$q = C \Delta T$$ A large heat capacity means a given amount of heat results in only a small temperature rise.

subsections.title: Molar and Specific Heat Capacity
subsections.content_text: Heat capacity is directly proportional to the amount of substance. Molar heat capacity ($C_m$) is the heat capacity for one mole of a substance, defined as $C_m = \frac{C}{n}$. It is the quantity of heat needed to raise the temperature of one mole by one degree Celsius (or one Kelvin). Specific heat, also called specific heat capacity is the quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of one unit mass of a substance by one degree celsius (or one kelvin). For finding out the heat, $q$, required to raise the temperatures of a sample, we multiply the specific heat of the substance, $c$, by the mass $m$, and temperatures change, $\Delta T$ as $q = c \times m \times \Delta T = C \Delta T$. This relationship allows for the calculation of heat exchange based on the measurable temperature change and the known thermal properties of the material.

subsections.title: The Relationship between $C_p$ and $C_v$ for an Ideal Gas
subsections.content_text: At constant volume, the heat capacity is denoted by $C_v$, and at constant pressure, it is denoted by $C_p$. We can write the equations for heat, $q$, as $q_v = C_v \Delta T = \Delta U$ at constant volume, and $q_p = C_p \Delta T = \Delta H$ at constant pressure. For one mole of an ideal gas, the relationship between enthalpy and internal energy is $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(pV)$. Since $pV = RT$ for one mole, this becomes $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(RT) = \Delta U + R \Delta T$. Substituting the heat capacity expressions, we get $C_p \Delta T = C_v \Delta T + R \Delta T$. Dividing by $\Delta T$ yields $C_p = C_v + R$, or $C_p - C_v = R$.

sections.title: MEASUREMENT OF $\Delta U$ AND $\Delta H$: CALORIMETRY

subsections.title: Introduction to Calorimetry
subsections.content_text: Energy changes associated with chemical or physical processes can be measured using an experimental technique called calorimetry. In calorimetry, the process is carried out in a vessel called a calorimeter, which is immersed in a known volume of a liquid. By knowing the heat capacity of the liquid and the calorimeter, the heat evolved in the process can be determined by measuring temperature changes. Measurements are typically made under two conditions: i) at constant volume ($q_v$) and ii) at constant pressure ($q_p$).

subsections.title: $\Delta U$ Measurements
subsections.content_text: For chemical reactions, heat absorbed at constant volume is measured in a bomb calorimeter. A steel vessel (the bomb) is immersed in a water bath to ensure no heat is lost to the surroundings. A combustible substance is burnt in pure dioxygen supplied in the steel bomb. Heat evolved during the reaction is transferred to the water around the bomb and its temperature is monitored. Since the bomb calorimeter is sealed, its volume does not change ($\Delta V = 0$), meaning no work is done. Thus, the energy changes measured at constant volume correspond to $q_v = \Delta U$. The temperature change is converted to $q_v$ using the known heat capacity of the calorimeter.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.7
diagrams.description: A schematic diagram of a bomb calorimeter showing a steel bomb containing a sample and oxygen under pressure, immersed in a water bath. The setup includes a stirrer, a thermometer, firing leads for ignition, and an oxygen inlet.

subsections.title: $\Delta H$ Measurements
subsections.content_text: Measurement of heat change at constant pressure (generally under atmospheric pressure) can be done in a simpler calorimeter. We know that $\Delta H = q_p$ at constant pressure. In an exothermic reaction, heat is evolved and the system loses heat to the surroundings, making $q_p$ and $\Delta_r H$ negative. Conversely, in an endothermic reaction, heat is absorbed, making $q_p$ and $\Delta_r H$ positive.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.8
diagrams.description: A simple calorimeter for measuring heat changes at constant pressure, consisting of a foamed polystyrene cup containing a reaction mixture, equipped with a thermometer and a stirrer.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Combustion of Graphite
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates how to calculate the enthalpy change of a reaction using data from a bomb calorimeter experiment.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.6
worked_examples.problem: 1g of graphite is burnt in a bomb calorimeter in excess of oxygen at 298 K and 1 atmospheric pressure according to the equation $C (graphite) + O_2 (g) \rightarrow CO_2 (g)$. During the reaction, temperature rises from 298 K to 299 K. If the heat capacity of the bomb calorimeter is 20.7 kJ/K, what is the enthalpy change for the above reaction at 298 K and 1 atm?
worked_examples.solution: Suppose $q$ is the quantity of heat from the reaction mixture and $C_v$ is the heat capacity of the calorimeter. The quantity of heat absorbed by the calorimeter is $q = C_v \times \Delta T$. The heat from the reaction will have the same magnitude but opposite sign: $q = -C_v \times \Delta T = -20.7 \text{ kJ/K} \times (299 - 298) \text{ K} = -20.7 \text{ kJ}$. This is $\Delta U$ for the combustion of 1g of graphite. For combustion of 1 mol of graphite (12.0 g/mol): $\Delta U = \frac{12.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \times (-20.7 \text{ kJ})}{1 \text{ g}} = -2.48 \times 10^2 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Since $\Delta n_g = 0$ for the reaction $C(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g)$, $\Delta H = \Delta U = -2.48 \times 10^2 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

sections.title: ENTHALPY CHANGE, $\Delta_r H$ OF A REACTION – REACTION ENTHALPY

subsections.title: Reaction Enthalpy Definition
subsections.content_text: In a chemical reaction, reactants are converted into products. The enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction is called the reaction enthalpy, denoted by $\Delta_r H$. It is calculated as the difference between the sum of enthalpies of products and the sum of enthalpies of reactants: $\Delta_r H = \sum_i a_i H_{products} - \sum_i b_i H_{reactants}$, where $a_i$ and $b_i$ are the stoichiometric coefficients of the products and reactants respectively in the balanced chemical equation.

subsections.title: Standard Enthalpy of Reactions
subsections.content_text: The enthalpy of a reaction depends on the conditions under which it is carried out. The standard enthalpy of reaction ($\Delta_r H^\ominus$) is the enthalpy change for a reaction when all the participating substances are in their standard states. The standard state of a substance at a specified temperature is its pure form at 1 bar. For example, the standard state of liquid ethanol at 298 K is pure liquid ethanol at 1 bar. Standard conditions are denoted by the superscript $\ominus$.

subsections.title: Enthalpy Changes during Phase Transformations
subsections.content_text: Phase transformations involve energy changes. The enthalpy change that accompanies the melting of one mole of a solid substance in its standard state is called the standard enthalpy of fusion or molar enthalpy of fusion, $\Delta_{fus} H^\ominus$. For example, $H_2O(s) \rightarrow H_2O(l); \Delta_{fus} H^\ominus = 6.00 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Melting is an endothermic process, so all enthalpies of fusion are positive. Similarly, vaporization and other phase changes have characteristic standard enthalpy changes.

tables.label: Table 5.1
tables.caption: Standard Enthalpy Changes of Fusion and Vaporisation

subsections.title: Standard Enthalpy of Vaporization
subsections.content_text: The process of vaporization requires heat to overcome intermolecular forces in the liquid phase. At a constant temperature (the boiling point $T_b$) and constant pressure, the enthalpy change for the conversion of a liquid to its gas is known as the enthalpy of vaporization. For example, the vaporization of water is represented as: $H_2O(l) \rightarrow H_2O(g); \Delta_{vap}H^\ominus = + 40.79 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.\n\n**Definition:** The amount of heat required to vaporize one mole of a liquid at constant temperature and under standard pressure (1 bar) is called its **standard enthalpy of vaporization** or **molar enthalpy of vaporization**, $\Delta_{vap}H^\ominus$.

subsections.title: Standard Enthalpy of Sublimation
subsections.content_text: Sublimation is the direct conversion of a solid into its vapor without passing through the liquid state. Examples include the sublimation of solid $CO_2$ ('dry ice') at 195 K ($\Delta_{sub}H^\ominus = 25.2 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$) and the slow sublimation of naphthalene ($\Delta_{sub}H^\ominus = 73.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$).\n\n**Definition:** The **standard enthalpy of sublimation**, $\Delta_{sub}H^\ominus$, is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a solid substance sublimes at a constant temperature and under standard pressure (1 bar).

subsections.title: Factors Influencing Enthalpy of Phase Transformations
subsections.content_text: The magnitude of the enthalpy change during phase transformations depends significantly on the strength of the intermolecular interactions within the substance. For instance, water molecules are held together by strong hydrogen bonds in the liquid phase, requiring more heat to vaporize compared to organic liquids like acetone, where intermolecular dipole-dipole interactions are much weaker. Consequently, the enthalpy of vaporization for water is higher than that for acetone.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Heat of Evaporation and Internal Energy
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates the calculation of heat required to evaporate a specific mass of water and the determination of the corresponding internal energy change using the relationship between enthalpy and internal energy.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.7
worked_examples.problem: A swimmer coming out from a pool is covered with a film of water weighing about 18g. How much heat must be supplied to evaporate this water at 298 K? Calculate the internal energy of vaporisation at 298K. $\Delta_{vap}H^\ominus$ for water at 298K = $44.01 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.
worked_examples.solution: We can represent the process of evaporation as: $H_2O(l) \xrightarrow{\text{vaporisation}} H_2O(g)$. Number of moles in 18 g $H_2O(l)$ is: $n = \frac{18 \text{ g}}{18 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 1 \text{ mol}$. Heat supplied to evaporate 18g water at 298 K: $q = n \times \Delta_{vap}H^\ominus = (1 \text{ mol}) \times (44.01 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}) = 44.01 \text{ kJ}$. Assuming steam behaves as an ideal gas, the internal energy change is: $\Delta_{vap}U = \Delta_{vap}H^\ominus - p\Delta V = \Delta_{vap}H^\ominus - \Delta n_g RT$. $\Delta_{vap}U = 44.01 \text{ kJ} - (1)(8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1})(298 \text{ K})(10^{-3} \text{ kJ J}^{-1}) = 44.01 \text{ kJ} - 2.48 \text{ kJ} = 41.53 \text{ kJ}$.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Enthalpy and Internal Energy Change in Phase Transition
subsections.content_text: This example illustrates how to calculate the total enthalpy change for a multi-step process involving cooling and freezing, and how to relate it to the internal energy change when volume changes are negligible.

worked_examples.label: Problem 5.8
worked_examples.problem: Assuming the water vapour to be a perfect gas, calculate the internal energy change when 1 mol of water at 100°C and 1 bar pressure is converted to ice at 0°C. Given the enthalpy of fusion of ice is $6.00 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and heat capacity of water is $4.2 \text{ J/g}^\circ\text{C}$.
worked_examples.solution: The change takes place in two steps: Step 1: Cooling 1 mol $H_2O(l)$ from 100°C to 0°C. Enthalpy change $\Delta H_1 = -(18 \text{ g} \times 4.2 \text{ J/g}^\circ\text{C} \times 100^\circ\text{C}) = -7560 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -7.56 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Step 2: Freezing 1 mol $H_2O(l)$ at 0°C to ice at 0°C. Enthalpy change $\Delta H_2 = -6.00 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ (negative because heat is released during freezing). Total enthalpy change: $\Delta H = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 = -7.56 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} + (-6.00 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}) = -13.56 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Since there is negligible change in volume during the transition from liquid to solid, $p\Delta V = \Delta n_g RT = 0$. Therefore, $\Delta H = \Delta U = -13.56 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Standard Enthalpy of Formation
subsections.content_text: The **Standard Molar Enthalpy of Formation** ($\Delta_f H^\ominus$) is the enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its constituent elements in their most stable states of aggregation, known as **reference states**. The reference state of an element is its most stable state at 25°C (298.15 K) and 1 bar pressure. For example, the reference state for dihydrogen is $H_2$ gas, for dioxygen is $O_2$ gas, for carbon is $C_{graphite}$, and for sulphur is $S_{rhombic}$. By convention, the standard enthalpy of formation ($\Delta_f H^\ominus$) of an element in its reference state is taken as zero. Some examples of formation reactions are: 1. $H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l); \Delta_f H^\ominus = -285.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. 2. $C(graphite, s) + 2H_2(g) \rightarrow CH_4(g); \Delta_f H^\ominus = -74.81 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. 3. $2C(graphite, s) + 3H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow C_2H_5OH(l); \Delta_f H^\ominus = -277.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. It is important to note that $\Delta_f H^\ominus$ is a special case of reaction enthalpy where exactly one mole of the compound is formed from its elements.

tables.label: Table 5.2
tables.caption: Standard Molar Enthalpies of Formation (Δf H°) at 298K of a Few Selected Substances

subsections.title: Calculating Reaction Enthalpy from Enthalpies of Formation
subsections.content_text: The standard enthalpy of any reaction can be calculated if the standard enthalpies of formation of all reactants and products are known. The general equation is: $\Delta_r H^\ominus = \sum_i a_i \Delta_f H^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum_i b_i \Delta_f H^\ominus(\text{reactants})$ (5.15), where $a_i$ and $b_i$ represent the stoichiometric coefficients of the products and reactants in the balanced chemical equation.

worked_examples.label: Example: Decomposition of Calcium Carbonate
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the standard enthalpy change for the decomposition of calcium carbonate into lime and carbon dioxide: $CaCO_3(s) \rightarrow CaO(s) + CO_2(g)$ using the data from Table 5.2.
worked_examples.solution: Using the equation $\Delta_r H^\ominus = \sum a_i \Delta_f H^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum b_i \Delta_f H^\ominus(\text{reactants})$: $\Delta_r H^\ominus = [\Delta_f H^\ominus(CaO(s)) + \Delta_f H^\ominus(CO_2(g))] - [\Delta_f H^\ominus(CaCO_3(s))]$. From Table 5.2: $\Delta_f H^\ominus(CaO(s)) = -635.1 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, $\Delta_f H^\ominus(CO_2(g)) = -393.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, $\Delta_f H^\ominus(CaCO_3(s)) = -1206.9 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. $\Delta_r H^\ominus = [1(-635.1) + 1(-393.5)] - 1(-1206.9) = -1028.6 + 1206.9 = 178.3 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Thus, the decomposition is an endothermic process.

subsections.title: Thermochemical Equations
subsections.content_text:
A balanced chemical equation that includes the value of its enthalpy change ($\Delta_r H$) is called a **thermochemical equation**. It specifies the physical state (and allotropic state, if applicable) of each substance. For example: $C_2H_5OH(l) + 3O_2(g) ightarrow 2CO_2(g) + 3H_2O(l); \Delta_r H^\ominus = -1367 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. This equation describes the combustion of liquid ethanol. The negative sign indicates an exothermic reaction.

**Conventions for Thermochemical Equations:**
1. The coefficients in a balanced thermochemical equation refer to the number of moles of reactants and products involved in the reaction.
2. The numerical value of $\Delta_r H^\ominus$ refers to the number of moles of substances specified by the equation. Standard enthalpy change $\Delta_r H^\ominus$ will have units as $	ext{kJ mol}^{-1}$. The value of $\Delta_r H^\ominus$ refers to the number of moles of substances specified by the equation. Standard enthalpy change $\Delta_r H^\ominus$ will have units as $	ext{kJ mol}^{-1}$. To illustrate the concept, consider the calculation of heat of reaction for the following reaction: $Fe_2O_3(s) + 3H_2(g) ightarrow 2Fe(s) + 3H_2O(l)$. From the standard enthalpy of formation ($\Delta_f H^\ominus$) values, we find: $\Delta_f H^\ominus(H_2O, l) = -285.83 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $\Delta_f H^\ominus(Fe_2O_3, s) = -824.2 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. By convention, $\Delta_f H^\ominus(Fe, s) = 0$ and $\Delta_f H^\ominus(H_2, g) = 0$. Then, $\Delta_r H^\ominus = 3(-285.83 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1}) - 1(-824.2 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1}) = (-857.5 + 824.2) 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1} = -33.3 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.
The coefficients used in thermochemical calculations are pure numbers equal to the respective stoichiometric coefficients. The unit for $\Delta_r H^\ominus$ is $	ext{kJ mol}^{-1}$, which means 'per mole of reaction'. Balancing a chemical equation in a particular way defines the mole of reaction. If the equation is balanced differently, for example, $ rac{1}{2} Fe_2O_3(s) +  rac{3}{2} H_2(g) ightarrow Fe(s) +  rac{3}{2} H_2O(l)$, then $\Delta_r H^\ominus$ would be $ rac{1}{2} \Delta_r H_1^\ominus = -16.6 	ext{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, showing that enthalpy is an extensive quantity. Additionally, when a chemical equation is reversed, the value of $\Delta_r H^\ominus$ is reversed in sign. For example, $N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) ightarrow 2NH_3(g)$ has $\Delta_r H^\ominus = -91.8 	ext{ kJ}$, while $2NH_3(g) ightarrow N_2(g) + 3H_2(g)$ has $\Delta_r H^\ominus = +91.8 	ext{ kJ}$.

subsections.title: Hess’s Law of Constant Heat Summation
subsections.content_text: Enthalpy is a state function, so the change in enthalpy is independent of the path between the initial state (reactants) and final state (products). Hess’s Law states: 'If a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.' This law is crucial for calculating enthalpy changes that cannot be measured directly.

subsections.title: Application of Hess’s Law: Formation of Carbon Monoxide
subsections.content_text: Consider the enthalpy change for $C(graphite, s) + \frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow CO(g)$. Since $CO_2$ is always produced alongside $CO$, direct measurement is difficult. However, using related reactions: (i) $C(graphite, s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g); \Delta_r H^\ominus = -393.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and (ii) $CO(g) + \frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g); \Delta_r H^\ominus = -283.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. To get the desired equation, we reverse (ii) to get (iii) $CO_2(g) \rightarrow CO(g) + \frac{1}{2} O_2(g); \Delta_r H^\ominus = +283.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Adding (i) and (iii) gives the target reaction with $\Delta_r H^\ominus = (-393.5 + 283.0) = -110.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Generalization and Diagrammatic Representation of Hess’s Law
subsections.content_text: In general, if enthalpy of an overall reaction $A \rightarrow B$ along one route is $\Delta_r H$ and along another route via intermediates $C$ and $D$ is $\Delta_r H_1, \Delta_r H_2, \Delta_r H_3$, then $\Delta_r H = \Delta_r H_1 + \Delta_r H_2 + \Delta_r H_3$. This relationship is often represented in a cycle or path diagram showing that the total enthalpy change is the same regardless of the steps taken.

diagrams.label: Fig. 5.x
diagrams.description: A schematic diagram showing two paths from state A to state B. Path 1 is a direct arrow from A to B labeled $\Delta_r H$. Path 2 consists of three steps: A to C ($\Delta H_1$), C to D ($\Delta H_2$), and D to B ($\Delta H_3$). The diagram illustrates that the sum of enthalpies in Path 2 equals the enthalpy of Path 1.

sections.title: ENTHALPIES FOR DIFFERENT TYPES OF REACTIONS

subsections.title: Standard Enthalpy of Combustion ($\Delta_c H^\ominus$)
subsections.content_text: Standard enthalpy of combustion is defined as the enthalpy change per mole (or per unit amount) of a substance when it undergoes complete combustion and all the reactants and products being in their standard states at the specified temperature. Combustion reactions are exothermic. For example, the combustion of butane ($C_4H_{10}$) releases $2658 \text{ kJ}$ of heat: $C_4H_{10}(g) + \frac{13}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow 4CO_2(g) + 5H_2O(l); \Delta_c H^\ominus = -2658.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Similarly, combustion of glucose gives out $2802.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$ of heat. Our body also generates energy from food by the same overall process as combustion, although the final products are produced after a series of complex bio-chemical reactions involving enzymes.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Standard Enthalpy of Formation of Benzene
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates how to calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of benzene using its enthalpy of combustion and the enthalpies of formation of its combustion products ($CO_2$ and $H_2O$). The process involves setting up thermochemical equations for each known value and combining them using Hess's Law to match the target formation reaction: $6C(graphite) + 3H_2(g) \rightarrow C_6H_6(l)$.

worked_examples.label: Example 5.9
worked_examples.problem: The combustion of one mole of benzene takes place at $298 \text{ K}$ and $1 \text{ atm}$. After combustion, $CO_2(g)$ and $H_2O(l)$ are produced and $3267.0 \text{ kJ}$ of heat is liberated. Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation, $\Delta_f H^\ominus$ of benzene. Standard enthalpies of formation of $CO_2(g)$ and $H_2O(l)$ are $-393.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $-285.83 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
worked_examples.solution: The formation reaction of benzene is: $6C(graphite) + 3H_2(g) \rightarrow C_6H_6(l); \Delta_f H^\ominus = ?$ ... (i). The enthalpy of combustion of 1 mol of benzene is: $C_6H_6(l) + \frac{15}{2} O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2(g) + 3H_2O(l); \Delta_c H^\ominus = -3267 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ ... (ii). The enthalpy of formation of 1 mol of $CO_2(g)$ is: $C(graphite) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g); \Delta_f H^\ominus = -393.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ ... (iii). The enthalpy of formation of 1 mol of $H_2O(l)$ is: $H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l); \Delta_c H^\ominus = -285.83 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ ... (iv). Multiplying eqn. (iii) by 6 and eqn. (iv) by 3 we get: $6C(graphite) + 6O_2(g) \rightarrow 6CO_2(g); \Delta_f H^\ominus = -2361 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $3H_2(g) + \frac{3}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow 3H_2O(l); \Delta_f H^\ominus = -857.49 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Summing these two equations: $6C(graphite) + 3H_2(g) + \frac{15}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow 6CO_2(g) + 3H_2O(l); \Delta_r H^\ominus = -3218.49 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ ... (v). Reversing equation (ii): $6CO_2(g) + 3H_2O(l) \rightarrow C_6H_6(l) + \frac{15}{2} O_2; \Delta_r H^\ominus = +3267.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ ... (vi). Adding equations (v) and (vi), we get: $6C(graphite) + 3H_2(g) \rightarrow C_6H_6(l); \Delta_f H^\ominus = +48.51 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Enthalpy of Atomization ($\Delta_a H^\ominus$)
subsections.content_text: Enthalpy of atomization is the enthalpy change on breaking one mole of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the gas phase. For example, in the atomization of dihydrogen: $H_2(g) \rightarrow 2H(g); \Delta_a H^\ominus = 435.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Here, H atoms are formed by breaking H-H bonds. For polyatomic molecules like methane: $CH_4(g) \rightarrow C(g) + 4H(g); \Delta_a H^\ominus = 1665 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. For metals, the enthalpy of atomization is the same as the enthalpy of sublimation, e.g., $Na(s) \rightarrow Na(g); \Delta_a H^\ominus = 108.4 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

subsections.title: Bond Enthalpy ($\Delta_{bond} H^\ominus$): Diatomic Molecules
subsections.content_text: Chemical reactions involve breaking and making chemical bonds. Energy is required to break a bond and released when one is formed. Two terms are used in thermodynamics: (i) Bond dissociation enthalpy and (ii) Mean bond enthalpy. For diatomic molecules, bond dissociation enthalpy is the enthalpy change when one mole of covalent bonds of a gaseous covalent compound is broken to form products in the gas phase. For example: $Cl_2(g) \rightarrow 2Cl(g); \Delta_{Cl-Cl} H^\ominus = 242 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $O_2(g) \rightarrow 2O(g); \Delta_{O=O} H^\ominus = 428 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. For diatomic molecules, bond dissociation enthalpy is identical to the enthalpy of atomization.

subsections.title: Bond Enthalpy: Polyatomic Molecules
subsections.content_text:
In polyatomic molecules, bond dissociation enthalpy can differ for different bonds within the same molecule. For methane ($CH_4$), the overall thermochemical equation for its atomization is: $CH_4(g) \rightarrow C(g) + 4H(g); \Delta_a H^\ominus = 1665 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Although all four C-H bonds in methane are identical in length and energy, the energies required to break the individual C-H bonds in each successive step differ : In polyatomic molecules, the energy required to break identical bonds in successive steps can differ. For example, in methane ($CH_4$), the four C-H bonds are identical in length and energy, but the bond dissociation enthalpy for each step is different:
1. $CH_4(g) ightarrow CH_3(g) + H(g); \Delta_{bond}H^\ominus = +427 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
2. $CH_3(g) ightarrow CH_2(g) + H(g); \Delta_{bond}H^\ominus = +439 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
3. $CH_2(g) ightarrow CH(g) + H(g); \Delta_{bond}H^\ominus = +452 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
4. $CH(g) ightarrow C(g) + H(g); \Delta_{bond}H^\ominus = +347 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$

The total enthalpy change for the atomization of methane is the sum of these steps: $CH_4(g) \rightarrow C(g) + 4H(g); \Delta_a H^\ominus = 1665 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. In such cases, we use the **mean bond enthalpy** (also called average bond enthalpy), calculated as the average of the individual bond dissociation enthalpies. For the C-H bond in methane: $\Delta_{C-H}H^\ominus = \frac{1}{4} (1665 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}) = 416 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. Mean bond enthalpies for the same bond type can vary slightly between different compounds (e.g., $CH_3CH_2Cl$ vs $CH_3NO_2$) but are generally consistent enough for predictive calculations.

subsections.title: Reaction Enthalpy from Bond Enthalpies
subsections.content_text:
Reaction enthalpies are critical quantities arising from the breaking of old bonds and the formation of new ones. In the gas phase, the standard enthalpy of reaction, $\Delta_r H^\ominus$, can be predicted if the bond enthalpies of all reactants and products are known. The relationship is given by:
$$\Delta_r H^\ominus = \sum \text{bond enthalpies}_{reactants} - \sum \text{bond enthalpies}_{products}$$
This relationship is particularly useful when standard enthalpies of formation ($\Delta_f H^\ominus$) are unavailable. It is based on the principle that the net enthalpy change is the energy required to break all reactant bonds minus the energy released when product bonds are formed. Note that this relationship is an approximation and is strictly valid only when all substances (reactants and products) are in the gaseous state.
